93rd Constitutional
Amendment Act,
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The office of **Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities (Article 350B)** was established by the **7th** **Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956.** The 70th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 amended Articles 54 and 239AA to include elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of the National Capital Territory (NCT) of Delhi and the Union Territory of Puducherry in the electoral college for the Presidential election. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992** inserted Article 243-I, which mandates the Governor to constitute a **State Finance Commission (SFC)** every five years. The main objective of the SFC is to discern and recommend the ways and means to improve and invent the new sources of income of Panchayats and recommend the formula of distribution of funds from the State divisible pool to the Municipalities and Panchayats. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **GST Council** is a constitutional body established under Article 279A. It was constituted by the President, within sixty days from the date of commencement of the **101st Constitutional** **Amendment Act, 2016,** as mandated by the Article 279A. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● The 102nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2018 granted the Constitutional Status to the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) under article 338B. The Commission consists of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, and three other members appointed by the President.
o Article 338 provided for a Special Officer (Commissioner for SCs and STs). The 65th
Constitutional Amendment Act, 1990 replaced the single special officer with a high-level multimember National Commission for SCs and STs. Later **89th Constitutional Amendment Act,** **2003** bifurcated it into two separate bodies: o National Commission for Scheduled Castes (Article 338) o National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (Article 338A). **So, pair IV is not correctly**
**matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) seeks Kerala Government’s response on alleged religion-based OBC reservations.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is correct
with regard to the Constitution of India?

options.a:
It has declared English to be one of the
national languages.
options.b:
Hindi, written in the Devanagari script, shall
be the official language of the Union.
options.c:
States have to mandatorily conduct all official
businesses exclusively in their respective
State language.
options.d:
All proceedings in the High Courts are
required to be conducted in English language
only.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There is no provision in the Constitution that designates any language as the National Language. **So,** **option (a) is not correct.**
● As part of the Munshi-Ayyangar formula, Article 343(1) of the Constitution as adopted in 1950 said, **“The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script.** The form of numerals to be used for the official purposes of the Union shall be the international form of Indian numerals. **So,** **option (b) is correct.**
● The Constitution allows the respective State Legislatures (Article 345) and the President (Article 347) to recognise some language or languages other than Hindi as the languages for intra-State official transactions. **So, option (c) is not correct.**
● With respect to the language of the courts, Article 348(1) lays down that until Parliament otherwise provides, proceedings in the Supreme Court and in a High Court are to be in the English language.
o However, Article 348(2) provides that the Governor of a State, with the previous consent of the
President, may authorise the use of Hindi, or any other official language of the State, in proceedings in the High Court, but not with respect to the judgments, decrees and orders passed by it, which shall be in the English language as required by Article 348(1). **So, option (d) is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** President Droupadi Murmu released the Constitution of India in the Santhali language, written in Ol Chiki script.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Big Bang theory, consider the following statements:
I. - The universe originated from a “singularity”, a point of infinite density and heat.
II. 	 The Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) represents the heat leftover from the Big Bang.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Modern cosmology explains the origin and evolution of the universe through the Big Bang theory, which is supported by multiple observational evidence. Two of the most important concepts associated with this theory are the idea of an initial singularity and the presence of cosmic background radiation.
● According to the **Big Bang theory**, the universe began about 13.8 billion years ago from an **extremely** **hot, dense state referred to as a “singularity”.** From this state, space, time, matter and energy began expanding, leading to the formation of the universe as observed today. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The **Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) is the remnant thermal radiation from the early** **universe, originating around 380,000 years post-Big Bang** when the universe cooled enough for atoms to form. This radiation represents the leftover heat from the Big Bang and is observed today as uniform microwave radiation filling the entire universe. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
With reference to the geological time scale, consider the following statements:
I. - Precambrian eon is the earliest and longest span of geological time.
II. 	 The majority of the Earth’s known coal deposits belong to the Carboniferous period.
III. 	 The Gondwana supercontinent broke up during the Jurassic period.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The geological time scale provides a chronological framework for understanding the evolution of the earth, its crust, life forms and major tectonic events. Different eons, eras and periods are marked by distinct geological and biological developments.
● **The Precambrian eon represents the earliest and longest span of geological time**, extending from the formation of the earth about 4.6 billion years ago to the beginning of the Cambrian period. It includes the Hadean, Archean and Proterozoic eras and accounts for nearly 88% of earth’s history. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Carboniferous Period is known as the Age of Coal**  because extensive swampy forests during this time led to the formation of the world’s largest coal deposits. A major proportion of the coal found today originated during this period. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Jurassic period witnessed the beginning of the breakup of the Gondwana** **supercontinent**, leading to the gradual separation of landmasses such as Africa, South America, Antarctica, Australia and India. This tectonic fragmentation played a crucial role in shaping present-day continental configurations. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to seismic waves, consider the following statements:
I. - Primary waves travel through both solids and liquids at the same speed.
II. 	 Secondary waves are totally absent in the core of the Earth.
III. 	 Surface waves cover the longest distances of all the seismic waves.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Seismic waves generated during earthquakes are classified into body waves and surface waves based on their mode of propagation. Their behaviour within the Earth’s interior provides critical information about the planet’s internal structure.
● Primary (P) waves are the fastest seismic waves and can travel through both solids and liquids, but their speed is not the same in all media. **P-waves travel faster in solids than in liquids** due to differences in elasticity and density. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Secondary (S) waves cannot travel through liquids. **Since the Earth’s outer core is in a molten** **(liquid) state, S-waves are completely absent in the core,** a fact that provided crucial evidence for the liquid nature of the outer core. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Surface waves are confined to the Earth’s surface and lose energy relatively quickly compared to body waves. **Although they cause maximum destruction near the surface, they do not travel the** **longest distances;** body waves can be recorded across the globe. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Geomorphic Force Description
I. - Epeirogenic Formation of broad continental uplifts and basins
II. - Orogenic Formation of fold mountains like the Himalayas
III. - Exogenic It involves processes like river erosion and deposition
Which of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Geomorphic processes are broadly classified into endogenetic and exogenetic forces based on their origin. These forces play a crucial role in shaping the Earth’s surface through vertical movements, horizontal movements, and surface-level processes.
● **Epeirogenic forces** cause slow, large-scale vertical movements of the Earth’s crust, **leading to the** **uplift or subsidence of extensive continental areas** and the formation of plateaus and basins without significant deformation. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Orogenic forces** involve intense **horizontal compressional forces that result in folding, faulting** **and mountain building**, such as the formation of the Himalayan fold mountains. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Exogenic forces** operate at or near the Earth’s surface and **include processes like river erosion,** **weathering, mass wasting and deposition,** which continuously modify landforms. **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
**Statement I:**
Most destructive tsunamis are generated by strong
undersea earthquakes occurring along convergent
plate boundaries.
**Statement II:**
Shallow-focus earthquakes occurring beneath the
ocean displaces massive volumes of overlying
seawater, creating long-wavelength waves.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Earthquakes and tsunamis are closely linked geophysical phenomena, particularly in tectonically active oceanic regions.
● **The world’s most powerful and destructive tsunamis**—such as the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami and the 2011 Japan tsunami—**originate mainly at convergent (subduction) plate boundaries.** At these boundaries, one tectonic plate is forced beneath another, leading to the accumulation of enormous stress. When this stress is suddenly released during a major earthquake, large-scale deformation of the seafloor occurs, which is ideal for tsunami generation. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Tsunamis are generated not merely by earthquake shaking, but by the vertical uplift or subsidence of the seafloor. **Shallow-focus earthquakes** (generally less than 70 km deep) **occurring beneath the** **ocean can abruptly raise or lower the ocean floor**. This sudden movement displaces massive volumes of overlying seawater, creating long-wavelength waves that propagate outward as tsunamis. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Convergent plate boundaries commonly produce shallow-focus, high-magnitude earthquakes with significant vertical seafloor displacement due to thrust faulting. The mechanism in turn generates undersea earthquakes at convergent margins that generate tsunamis. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Volcano Feature
I. - Hawaiian
Quiet eruption with
highly fluid basaltic
lava
II. - Strombolian
Intermittent explosive
eruption with lava
fountains
III. Vulcanian
Short, violent
explosions with ash and
lava blocks
IV. Pelean
Extremely explosive
eruption with glowing
clouds
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Volcanic eruptions are classified based on the nature of magma, eruptive mechanism and explosiveness. Each volcanic type represents a distinct eruptive behaviour associated with specific landforms and hazards.
● **Hawaiian-type eruptions** are characterized by **very gentle and quiet volcanic activity**. The magma involved is basaltic, having low silica content and very high temperature, which makes it extremely fluid. Because gases can escape easily, explosions are rare, and lava flows spread over large areas, forming broad shield volcanoes. Typical landforms include lava lakes and long, thin lava flows. Classic Hawaiiantype volcanoes are Mauna Loa and Kīlauea in Hawaii. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Strombolian eruptions** show **moderate explosivity and occur in the form of regular or** **intermittent bursts.** Gas pressure builds up in the magma and is released periodically, **producing** **lava fountains,** glowing cinders, volcanic bombs, and lapilli. These eruptions are more violent than Hawaiian ones but less destructive than Vulcanian or Pelean types. Stromboli (Italy), Parícutin (Mexico), and Mount Erebus (Antarctica) are  examples of this type. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Vulcanian eruptions are marked by short-lived but extremely violent explosions.** The magma is relatively viscous, which blocks the volcanic vent and allows pressure to build up. When the pressure is suddenly released, it results in powerful blasts that eject ash clouds, rock fragments, lava blocks, and bombs. These eruptions can be hazardous but are usually brief. The example of Mauna Loa is incorrect, as it is a Hawaiian-type shield volcano. **True Vulcanian examples include Vulcano (Italy) and** **Sakurajima (Japan).** **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Pelean eruptions** are among the **most destructive volcanic eruptions**. They are dominated by the formation and collapse of lava domes, producing fast-moving, superheated pyroclastic flows known as nuée ardente (glowing clouds). These flows rush down the slopes at very high speeds, burning and burying everything in their path. Mount Pelée in Martinique is the correct and classic example, especially noted for its catastrophic 1902 eruption. **Mayon Volcano (Philippines)** is known for frequent Peléan activity, including its 1968 eruption. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, all four of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Cordilleras consist of several mountain
groups and systems.
II. 	 Geosynclines are water depressions characterized by sedimentation.
III. 	 Cratons composed of sedimentary rocks are tectonically unstable parts of the Earth.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Large-scale mountain systems and structural depressions** play a crucial role in understanding orogenic processes and the evolution of the Earth’s crust. Concepts such as cordilleras and geosynclines are fundamental in geomorphology.
● **Cordilleras are extensive and complex mountain systems composed of several parallel** **mountain ranges, plateaus and intermontane basins,** such as the North American Cordillera,
which includes the Rockies, Sierra Nevada and Coast Ranges. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **A geosyncline is a long, narrow subsiding trough or depression, usually located in marine** **environments,** where thick sediments accumulate over long geological periods, later giving rise to folded mountain systems. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Cratons are the oldest, most stable parts of the Earth’s continental crust, forming the core** **of continents. They are composed mainly of ancient igneous and metamorphic** rocks and have remained tectonically stable for billions of years, largely unaffected by folding, faulting, or volcanic activity. Cratons are typically divided into shields, where the ancient rocks are exposed at the surface, and platforms, where these rocks are covered by a thin layer of sedimentary deposits. Because of their stability and antiquity, cratons often contain rich mineral resources such as iron ore, gold, and diamonds. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Lake Type
I. - Gurudongmar Glacial
II. - Loktak Tectonic
III. - Lonar Crater
IV. - Bhimtal Karst
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Lakes are classified based on their mode of origin, such as tectonic movements, glacial activity, river processes, volcanic activity and solution of rocks. Correct identification of lake types is important in physical geography and geomorphology.
● **Gurudongmar Lake,** located in northern **Sikkim** at a very high altitude, **is a glacial lake**. It has been formed due to glacial erosion and the accumulation of meltwater in a region strongly influenced by present and past glaciation. Such lakes are common in the Himalayan region. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **Loktak Lake in Manipur** is considered **a tectonic lake,** formed in a structural depression created by crustal movements. Over time, it has been modified by fluvial processes. The lake is famous for its floating islands called phumdis, but its basic origin is tectonic in nature. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Lonar Lake in Maharashtra is a classic crater lake,** formed due to a meteorite impact on basaltic rock. It is one of the world’s rare impact craters developed in basalt and is a textbook example of a crater (impact) lake. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Bhimtal Lake in Uttarakhand is primarily a tectonic lake,** formed due to faulting and structural movements in the Himalayan region. Karst lakes develop due to the solution of limestone rocks, which is not the dominant geological process involved in the formation of Bhimtal. **So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Delta Formed by River
I. - Arcuate Ganga–Brahmaputra
II. - Bird’s-foot Mississippi
III. - Cuspate Krishna
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Deltas are classified based on their shape and dominant geomorphic processes such as river deposition, wave action and tidal influence. Indian rivers display a variety of deltaic and estuarine forms depending on coastal conditions and sediment supply.
● **Arcuate deltas** are fan-shaped with a convex seaward margin, formed where river deposition dominates over wave action. The **Ganga–Brahmaputra delta,** the largest in the world, and the Mahanadi delta are classic examples of arcuate deltas in India. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Bird’s-foot deltas** are characterised by long, finger-like distributaries extending into the sea, with minimal wave reworking. This type is best exemplified by the Mississippi River. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **Cuspate deltas** have a pointed, tooth-like shape formed by strong wave action on either side of the river mouth. The **Subarnarekha delta** on the eastern coast of India is commonly cited as an example of a cuspate delta. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Landform Feature
I. - Inselberg Isolated residual hill rising abruptly from a plain
II. - Yardang Broad depositional plain formed by coalescence of alluvial fans
III. Barchan Crescent-shaped sand dune with horns pointing in the direction of wind movement
IV. Bajada Elongated, ridge-like features carved out of soft sedimentary rocks
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Arid and semi-arid regions are shaped predominantly by aeolian (wind-related) processes along with episodic fluvial action. The landforms produced in such environments reflect erosion, transportation and deposition by wind and water under conditions of scanty vegetation and low rainfall.
● **Inselbergs are isolated rock hills that stand prominently above surrounding plains.** They are remnants of resistant rock left behind after prolonged denudation and weathering in arid and semi-arid regions. Common examples include granite inselbergs found in African deserts. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **A bajada forms along the foothills of mountain ranges in arid regions when several adjacent** **alluvial fans merge together.** Though fluvial in origin, it is an important arid-region depositional landform. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
● **Barchans are the most common type of sand dunes formed in deserts where wind direction** **is constant and sand supply is limited**. Their crescent shape with horns pointing in the direction of wind movement is a diagnostic feature. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Yardangs are elongated, ridge-like features carved out of soft sedimentary rocks by** **persistent wind erosion, particularly abrasion and deflation.** They are aligned parallel to prevailing wind direction and are typical of hot deserts such as the Sahara and the Gobi. **So, pair II is** **not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Solar Radiation, Heat Balance, Temperature
questions.questionText:
With reference to the ocean stratification, consider the following statements:
I. - The maximum temperature is always found between 20 and 200 metres depth.
II. 	 At all latitudes, salinity decreases uniformly with increasing depth.
III. 	 The pycnocline layer is a transition layer where water density increases rapidly with depth.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The vertical structure of ocean water is characterized by variations in temperature, salinity and density with depth. These variations lead to the formation of distinct layers such as the mixed layer, thermocline, halocline and pycnocline, which differ across latitudes and ocean basins.
● **The highest ocean temperatures are generally observed at the surface layer due to direct** **solar radiation.** While a thermocline typically develops between about 20 and 200 metres in tropical and mid-latitude regions, this zone represents a rapid decrease in temperature with depth rather than the zone of maximum temperature. Therefore, maximum temperature is not always found between 20 and 200 metres. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Salinity does not decrease uniformly with depth at all latitudes.** In low latitudes, salinity may decrease with depth due to high evaporation at the surface, whereas in high latitudes, surface waters can be fresher due to ice melt and precipitation, sometimes resulting in increasing salinity with depth. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● **The pycnocline is a distinct transition layer where water density increases rapidly with** **depth.** It separates the lighter, warmer, and less dense surface waters from the colder, denser deep ocean waters. Density differences arise due to combined effects of temperature and salinity. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
With reference to marine deposits, consider the following statements:
I. - Terrigenous deposits are derived from the weathering and erosion of continental rocks.
II. 	 Pelagic deposits consist of the remains of marine plants and animals in the form of oozes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Marine deposits on the ocean floor are classified on the basis of their source and mode of origin. Terrigenous and pelagic deposits represent two major categories reflecting continental and deep-ocean contributions respectively.
● **Terrigenous deposits originate from the mechanical and chemical weathering of rocks on** **the continents.** The **eroded materials** are transported to the oceans by rivers, wind, glaciers and coastal erosion and are deposited mainly on continental shelves, slopes and rises. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Pelagic deposits are found in the deep ocean basins, far from continental influence.** They primarily consist of biogenic materials derived from the skeletal remains of marine organisms such as foraminifera, coccolithophores, radiolarians and diatoms. These deposits commonly occur as calcareous and siliceous oozes. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water on Earth Surface, Ocean Relief
questions.questionText:
Which of the following factors can modify ocean currents?
I. - Amount of insolation received at the Earth’s surface
II. 	 Configuration of continents and ocean basins
III. 	 Differences in salinity
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Ocean currents are large-scale movements of ocean water driven and modified by several physical and geographical factors. These currents play a vital role in heat redistribution, climate regulation and marine ecosystems.
● **The rotation of the Earth** gives rise to the Coriolis force, which deflects moving water to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere. This force is a major factor in determining the direction of major ocean currents.
● **Unequal insolation across latitudes** creates temperature differences between equatorial and polar waters. Warm water tends to move poleward while cold water moves equatorward, generating and modifying ocean currents. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The shape and orientation of continents and ocean basins obstruct, deflect and channel** **ocean currents.** This is why currents follow curved paths along continental margins rather than moving in straight lines. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Variations in salinity affect water density and, along with temperature, control** **thermohaline circulation.** High salinity increases density, causing water to sink and drive deep ocean currents. **So, point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the phenomenon of ‘Western Boundary Intensification’?

options.a: The concentration of ocean currents along the western margins of ocean basins due to coriolis force and prevailing wind systems
options.b: The strengthening of cold ocean currents along the western coasts of continents caused by upwelling and offshore winds
options.c: The seasonal heat waves along the western margin of the continents
options.d: The seasonal shifting of trade winds from the western margin of continents

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Western Boundary Intensification refers to the strengthening, narrowing, and acceleration** **of ocean currents along the western margins of ocean basins.**
● This happens due to the Coriolis force caused by Earth’s rotation, which pushes water toward the western side of oceans.
● As a result, currents like the Gulf Stream and Kuroshio become faster and deeper compared to eastern boundary currents, which are weaker and broader. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText: Which of the following phenomena are associated with the atmospheric layers of mesosphere and above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Certain atmospheric phenomena occur exclusively in the upper layers of the atmosphere due to unique physical conditions such as low air density, high ionization and interaction with solar radiation.
● **Auroras** are **luminous displays observed in the polar regions and occur mainly in the** **thermosphere and ionosphere.** They are produced when charged particles from the Sun collide with gases such as oxygen and nitrogen in the upper atmosphere, causing them to emit light.
● **Noctilucent clouds** are **rare, thin, high-altitude clouds found in the mesosphere at heights** **of about 80–85 km**. They are visible only during twilight conditions when the lower atmosphere is in darkness but the upper atmosphere is still illuminated by sunlight.
● The Cumulonimbus clouds, Cirrus clouds, Stratus clouds, Monsoon winds and Jet streams occur in the lower atmosphere, mainly the troposphere and lower stratosphere. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the effects of temperature inversion?
I. - Accumulation of pollutants
II. 	 Formation of fog and frost
III. 	 Intense cloud development
IV. 	 Absence of storms
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Temperature inversion** is a condition in which **air temperature increases with height** instead of decreasing. This abnormal vertical temperature distribution has significant implications for weather, climate, pollution and human activities.
● **During temperature inversion, warm air overlies cooler air near the surface, preventing** **vertical air movement.** This trapping effect leads to the accumulation of pollutants such as smoke and vehicular emissions near the ground, especially in cities. **Temperature inversion promotes the** **formation of fog, smog and frost.** In valleys, cold air becomes trapped beneath warmer air, leading to dense fog formation, particularly during winter nights and early mornings. **So, points I and II are** **correct**
● Temperature inversion suppresses convection rather than enhancing it. The stable air stratification inhibits upward movement of air, reducing cloud development and precipitation.  **So, point III is not** **correct**
● Temperature inversion indicates stable atmospheric conditions with little vertical air movement. Meteorologists use its presence to predict calm weather, reduced turbulence and absence of storms. **So, point IV is correct** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Pressure, Winds, Airmasses (Coriolis Force, Frictional Force, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following winds:
I. - Trade Winds and Polar Jet Streams
II. 	 Westerlies and Polar Winds
III. 	 Trade Winds and Polar Winds
IV. 	 Westerlies and Polar Jet Streams
Which of the above winds blow in the same direction?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Trade winds and polar winds blow in the **same general direction**, whereas westerlies blow in the opposite direction.
○ Trade winds are **easterly winds** that blow from the subtropical high-pressure belts towards the equatorial low-pressure belt; in the Northern Hemisphere, they blow from the **north-east to** **the south-west**, and in the Southern Hemisphere from the **south-east to the north-west**, meaning they move from **east to west**. ○ Polar winds, also called **polar easterlies**, originate from the polar high-pressure areas and blow towards the sub-polar low-pressure belts; due to the Coriolis force, they too flow from **east to** **west**.
● In contrast, westerlies blow from the subtropical high-pressure belts towards the sub-polar low-pressure belts and move from **west to east**, which is opposite to the direction of both trade winds and polar winds.
● The **polar jet stream blows primarily from west to east (westerly)** in both the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Located in the upper troposphere (about km high), these fast-moving, meandering air currents are caused by the Earth's rotation and strong temperature contrasts between polar and, warmer, mid-latitude air. **So, Trade Winds and Polar Winds blow in the east to west direction and Westerlies and Polar Jet** **Streams blow in the west to east direction.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With regard to the atmosphere, the Southern
Oscillation refers to:

options.a:
Periodic reversal of surface ocean currents in
the Indian Ocean driven by monsoon winds
options.b:
Coherent interannual fluctuation of
atmospheric pressure over the tropical Indo-
Pacific region
options.c:
Gradual migration of the Intertropical
Convergence Zone (ITCZ) caused by solar
radiation changes combined with jet stream
oscillations
options.d:
Cyclic shift in mid-latitude pressure belts
that indirectly alters tropical trade winds and
ocean temperatures

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Southern Oscillation (SO)** is a large-scale atmospheric phenomenon involving see-saw fluctuations in air pressure between the eastern and western tropical Pacific Ocean. When pressure is high over the eastern Pacific (near Peru), it is usually low over the western Pacific (near Indonesia and Australia), and vice versa. This pressure variation occurs interannually (every few years) and is closely linked with sea surface temperature changes in the Pacific.
● Together with oceanic changes (El Niño/La Niña), it forms the combined phenomenon known as ENSO (El Niño–Southern Oscillation). Thus, Southern Oscillation specifically refers to the atmospheric pressure component, not ocean currents alone. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to atmospheric disturbances,
consider the following statements:
I. - Cyclones are large-scale low-pressure
systems with spiral winds and widespread
rainfall.
II. 	 Thunderstorms are localized convective
storms associated with lightning and heavy
rain.
III. 	 Tornadoes are narrow, violently rotating
columns of air extending from cumulonimbus
clouds.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Cyclones, thunderstorms and tornadoes are atmospheric disturbances that differ significantly in scale, intensity, duration and associated weather conditions.
● **Cyclones are extensive low-pressure systems characterized by inward-spiraling winds,** **cloudiness and widespread precipitation.** Tropical cyclones derive energy from warm ocean waters and can affect vast coastal regions. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Thunderstorms are short-lived, localized convective systems formed due to intense surface** **heating and atmospheric instability.** They are marked by cumulonimbus clouds, heavy rainfall, lightning and thunder. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Tornadoes are extremely violent, narrow columns of rotating air that descend from** **cumulonimbus clouds,** often associated with severe thunderstorms. They have very high wind speeds and cause localized destruction. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Subsidiary Alliance introduced by the British was a direct extension and intensification of the earlier Ring Fence Policy.
**Statement II:** The Subsidiary Alliance effectively turned Indian states into dependent allies, undermining their sovereignty without formal annexation.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Subsidiary Alliance, introduced by Lord Wellesley, was a direct extension and** **intensification of the earlier Ring Fence Policy initiated by Warren Hastings**, both aimed at securing British frontiers by creating buffer states, but the Subsidiary Alliance brought greater direct control by making Indian states financially dependent and stationing British troops within them. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General of Bengal, introduced the Policy of Ring of Fence between 1765 and 1813 to secure the British East India Company’s frontiers. **It aimed at defending British** **territories by surrounding them with friendly or dependent Indian states**, thereby creating a protective “ring” against external threats such as Maratha confederacy, Mysore, and Afghan invasions. Under this policy, the ring-fence states were required to maintain subsidiary forces, which were organized, equipped, and commanded by Company officers, with the rulers of these states covering the expenses.
● The **Subsidiary Alliance** was a system of indirect control introduced by **Lord Wellesley** around **1798**, through which Indian rulers were compelled to accept British **troops (subsidiary forces)** stationed in their territories and pay for their maintenance. In return, the British offered military protection, but the allied ruler had to **surrender control over foreign relations**, disband their own armies, and often cede territory or revenue to meet the cost of the forces. This system effectively turned Indian states into **dependent allies**, undermining their sovereignty without formal annexation.
● While the Ring Fence created defensive buffers with allied states paying for defense, the Subsidiary Alliance under Wellesley made rulers accept permanent British forces and pay subsidies, effectively reducing them to dependent states and increasing British power. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: 1857 Revolt - Resistance & Revolt
questions.questionText: Which one of the following best describes the nature of the Revolt of 1857?

options.a: A revolt aimed at creating a modern nationstate
options.b: A spontaneous uprising among police forces due to pay disparities
options.c: A revolt marked by regional centres of resistance shaped by local grievances
options.d: A purely religious uprising directed against Western education and reforms

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **T**he Revolt of 1857 did not have the objective of forming a modern, unified nation-state. Rebel leaders generally sought the **restoration of traditional political authority**, such as the Mughal emperor in Delhi or displaced regional rulers, rather than a new nationalist political order.
● **T**he revolt **began as a sepoy mutiny** in the Bengal Army, not among civilian police. Although peasants participated in several regions, the civilian police neither initiated nor led the uprising.
● The Revolt of 1857 spread across north and central India and developed **regional centres of** **resistance** such as Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow, Jhansi, and Arrah. Each centre reflected **local political,** **economic, and social grievances**, and the revolt lacked centralized leadership or a common national programme.
● Religion was only **one of many factors** influencing the revolt. The uprising was driven by a combination of **political dispossession, economic exploitation, military grievances, and social anxieties**, and cannot be reduced to a purely religious movement. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Supreme Court, consider the following statements:
I. - It was established by the Regulating Act of 1773.
II. 	 It had civil, criminal, admiralty, and ecclesiastical jurisdiction and applied English law.
III. 	 The Act of Settlement, 1781, brought the Governor-General under its jurisdiction.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Supreme Court at Calcutta (1774)** was established under the **Regulating Act of 1773** to introduce a formal judicial system in British India and to check the arbitrary exercise of power by the East India Company. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It began functioning on **26 March 1774** with **Sir Elijah Impey** as the first Chief Justice and three puisne judges. The court had civil, criminal, admiralty, and ecclesiastical jurisdiction and applied **English law** to British subjects and, controversially, to Indians living in Calcutta. Its wide jurisdiction and conflict with the Governor-General and Council—most notably during the **trial of Raja Nand Kumar**—created serious administrative problems. These conflicts were later addressed by the **Act of Settlement** **(1781)**, which clarified the court’s jurisdiction and reduced friction between judicial and executive authorities. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Act of Settlement, 1781** (also called the **Amending Act of 1781**) was passed by the British Parliament to remove the administrative and judicial conflicts that had arisen between the **Supreme** **Court at Calcutta** and the **Governor-General in Council** after 1774. It clearly **defined and** **restricted the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court**, limiting it mainly to **British subjects** and **excluding the Governor-General, Council members, and Company official**s from its jurisdiction for acts done in their official capacity. The Act recognized the **authority of Indian laws** **and customs** in cases involving Indians and protected revenue officials from judicial interference while performing their duties. By doing so, it restored the Company’s administrative control and reduced friction between judicial and executive powers, bringing greater stability to colonial governance in Bengal. **So, statement III is not correct.** **So, only two of the statements given above are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tribal Uprising Region
I. - Chuar Rebellion Meghalaya
II. - Kol Rebellion Awadh
III. - Koya Rebellion Andhra
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Chuar Rebellion (1766–1799)** was one of the earliest tribal uprisings against British rule in **Bengal**. It was led by **tribal chiefs (Chuars)** and **dispossessed peasants** who were severely affected by the **new British revenue system**. The introduction of heavy land revenue demands, the displacement of traditional zamindars, and economic hardship caused widespread resentment.
○ The rebels attacked **Company officials, revenue offices, and symbols of British** **authority**. Although the rebellion was eventually suppressed, it exposed the harsh impact of British revenue policies on tribal society and marked the beginning of **organized tribal** **resistance** in colonial India. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● The **Kol Rebellion (1831–1832)** took place in the **Chotanagpur plateau** and involved tribal communities such as the **Hos, Mundas, and Oraons**. The uprising was triggered by the rapid intrusion of **moneylenders, zamindars, and British officials** into tribal areas, which led to the **alienation of** **tribal land** and the breakdown of traditional social systems. Exploitative practices like high rents, forced labour, and debt pushed the tribes into distress.
○ The rebels attacked **symbols of oppression**, including landlords and government establishments. Although the British eventually suppressed the revolt with military force, it revealed the deep resentment of tribal communities against colonial exploitation and loss of autonomy. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Koya Rebellion of 1879** occurred in the **Andhra region**, particularly in the **Godavari Agency** **areas**, and involved the **Koya tribe**, a forest-dwelling community. The revolt was mainly caused by **British forest laws, revenue demands, and interference in traditional tribal customs**. The imposition of restrictions on shifting cultivation, increased taxes, and the activities of moneylenders and officials threatened the Koyas’ livelihood and autonomy.
○ The rebels attacked **police stations, government offices, and symbols of colonial** **authority**. Although the uprising was eventually suppressed by British forces, it highlighted the growing tribal resistance to colonial policies and formed part of a broader pattern of **tribal** **revolts against British rule in the 19th century**. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** In the late 19th century, the Factory laws were introduced by the British government to regulate industrial labour in India.
**Statement II:** Cheap Indian labour gave Indian mills an advantage over British goods.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **First Factories Act, 1881**, was passed during the viceroyalty of **Lord Ripon**, and its **amendment in** **1891**. These were the **first attempts by the British government to regulate industrial labour** in India.
● By the late 19th century, Indian textile and jute mills—especially in **Bombay and Calcutta**—were employing large numbers of **women and children** under extremely harsh conditions. Working hours were long (often 12–14 hours a day), factories were unsafe, and wages were very low. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **British manufacturers** complained that cheap Indian labour gave Indian mills an **unfair advantage** over British goods. Therefore, the Acts were introduced both due to **humanitarian pressure** and **economic pressure from British industrialists**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Main Provisions of the Factories Act, 1881:** The Act applied mainly to factories using **mechanical** **power** and employing **100 or more workers**. Its key provisions were:
○ **Children below 7 years** were not allowed to work in factories ○ **Children between 7 and 12 years** could work only **9 hours a day** ○ **One hour rest** was compulsory during the working day ○ **Four holidays in a month** were required ○ Factories had to provide **basic safety and cleanliness** ○ However, it did **not apply to women**, and the law was very limited.
● **Factories Amendment Act, 1891:** Because the 1881 Act was weak, it was amended in **1891**, which: ○ Raised the **minimum age of children to 9 years** ○ Limited **working hours for women to 11 hours a day** ○ Improved **safety and health conditions**
● Although these laws were limited and mainly served British interests, they marked the **beginning of** **labour legislation in India**. They showed that the colonial state had begun to recognize that **unregulated industrial capitalism caused social suffering**, even though Indian workers still remained largely exploited. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation** **for Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText: Which of the following is correct with regard to the Local Self-Government Act of 1882?

options.a: Local bodies were to be composed largely of officials appointed by the District Magistrate.
options.b: Local bodies were fully dependent on government grants for their finances.
options.c: It neglected the self-governance needs of rural areas and focused mainly on urban areas.
options.d: Local bodies were placed under the general control of provincial governments.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Local Self-Government Act of 1882** was introduced by **Lord Ripon**, the Viceroy of India, and is considered a **milestone in the growth of democracy in India**. Its main aim was to involve Indians in the **administration of their own local affairs** and to train them in the principles of self-government.
● Under this Act, **local bodies** such as **municipalities, district boards and local boards** were set up in towns and rural areas. These bodies were to be composed largely of **elected Indian members**, although officials were still present. They were given responsibility for local matters such as **roads,** **water supply, sanitation, public health, education and local taxation**. The idea was that local problems should be solved by local people rather than distant British officials.
● Under the **Local Self-Government Act of 1882**, Lord Ripon not only created local bodies but also laid down rules regarding their **finances, supervision and composition**, so that they could function as genuine institutions of self-rule.
● **Finances**: The municipalities and district boards were given the power to raise their own funds through **local taxes, tolls, fees and rates**, and they also received **grants-in-aid from the government**. This ensured that local bodies were not completely dependent on the central government and could meet expenses for services like sanitation, roads, schools and water supply.
● **Supervision**: While these bodies were given considerable freedom in day-to-day administration, they were placed under the **general control of provincial governments**. Officials such as collectors and commissioners had the power to inspect accounts, approve budgets and intervene in cases of mismanagement, so that efficiency and financial discipline were maintained.
● **Composition**: Ripon’s scheme provided that the majority of members in local bodies should be **non-** **officials, and many of them elected**, rather than nominated by the government. This was a major step because it gave Indians real representation in local administration. Although British officials still held key positions, the Act marked the beginning of **democratic participation and decentralised** **governance** in India.
● The Act was important because it introduced the concept of **elected representation** and **decentralization of power**, which was new to British India. It created a foundation for **democratic** **institutions** and helped Indians gain experience in running public bodies. For this reason, Lord Ripon is often called the **“Father of Local Self-Government in India.”** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Applicable only to Bengali newspapers
II. 	 Criticizing British rule was considered
seditious
III. 	 The District Magistrate was the enforcement
authority
IV. 	 No right to appeal in courts
Which of the above were the provisions under the
Vernacular Press Act of 1878?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Vernacular Press Act was passed in 1878** by the British Indian Government under **Lord** **Lytton,** the Viceroy of India. Its main objective was to suppress the growing nationalist press in Indian languages. By the 1870s, many newspapers in Bengali, Hindi, Urdu, Marathi, and Tamil were criticizing: British economic exploitation; Famines and high taxation; Racial discrimination and British imperial policies. Papers like **Amrita Bazar Patrika, Kesari, Som Prakash, and Bharat Mihir** were spreading nationalist ideas among common people. The British feared this awakening and decided to curb it.
● The Vernacular Press Act applied to **all Indian-language (vernacular) newspapers** across India, not only Bengali. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Criticizing British rule was considered seditious.** Any writing that criticized the British government was treated as **“seditious”** under this Act. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The district magistrate was a nodal authority.** The **district magistrate** had wide powers to: ○ demand security, ○ confiscate the printing press, ○ stop publication. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Courts could not restore the printing press** as there was no right to appeal. The Act **denied the** **right to appeal in courts**. Magistrates could act without judicial interference. **So, point IV is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Ilbert Bill (1883) sought to remove racial
discrimination in the Indian judicial system.
**Statement II:**
Under the Criminal Procedure Code of 1873,
Indian magistrates were not allowed to try
European British subjects in criminal cases.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
 The **Ilbert Bill (1883)** was a controversial legal measure introduced by Lord Ripon, the Viceroy of
India, to remove racial discrimination in the Indian judicial system. At that time, Indian judges and magistrates were **not allowed to try European British subjects**, even if they were highly qualified. The Ilbert Bill proposed that **Indian district magistrates and session judges** should be given the same powers as British judges, including the authority to try **European offenders** in criminal cases. This was meant to establish **equality before the law**.  However, the Bill led to **violent opposition from the European community in India**, especially
British planters, merchants and officials, who refused to accept being tried by Indian judges. They organized protests, newspaper campaigns and public meetings, showing their strong racial prejudice. Due to this pressure, the British government modified the Bill, allowing Europeans to be tried by Indian judges only with the help of a **jury of which at least half had to be Europeans**.  **Criminal Procedure Code, 1873 (amended in 1875):** Under this law, **Indian magistrates were**
**not allowed to try European British subjects** in criminal cases. Even if an Indian judge was senior and highly qualified, a European accused had the right to be tried only by a **European judge or** **magistrate**. This provision made the judicial system **racially discriminatory** and placed Europeans above Indians in law. The **Ilbert Bill** was introduced to **amend this provision** and give Indian judges equal authority to try Europeans, but it faced strong opposition from the British community. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation** **for Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Deoband Movement :
I. - Its chief founders were Maulana Muhammad Qasim Nanautawi and Rashid Ahmad Gangohi.
II. 	 It kept itself away from the Indian freedom struggle focusing on religious and educational issues of Muslims.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Deoband **Movement** was one of the most important Islamic revivalist and reform movements in colonial India. It began in **1866** with the establishment of the **Dar-ul-Uloom at Deoband** in the Saharanpur district (present-day Uttar Pradesh). The movement arose in the aftermath of the **Revolt of** **1857**, when Muslim political power had collapsed and British rule was firmly established. Its founders believed that the decline of Muslims was due not only to British rule but also to moral decay, religious ignorance and the spread of un-Islamic practices.
● The chief founders were **Maulana Muhammad Qasim Nanautawi** and **Rashid Ahmad Gangohi**, both of whom had participated in the 1857 uprising. They aimed to **preserve and purify Islam** by returning to the original teachings of the **Qur’an and Hadith** and by eliminating practices they regarded as **innovations (bid‘at)** such as excessive saint-worship and ritualism influenced by Hindu customs. Unlike Aligarh reformers, they did not seek Western education or British patronage but focused on **traditional Islamic learning**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● While the **movement** initially focused more on religious and educational issues, it did eventually **align** **with the Indian National Congress** (INC). The movement **opposed Syed Ahmed Khan’s pro-** **British stance** and his approach of accommodating British rule and **supported the formation of the** **INC.** Over time, **Mahmud-ul-Hasan**, a prominent figure in the Deoband Movement, integrated **Islamic principles** with **Indian nationalist aspirations**. This helped give the Deobandi ideology a **political dimension** and laid the foundation for their support of the **Indian National Congress**. The Deoband Movement thus contributed to the **Indian freedom struggle**, although it was more cautious in its early stance. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText: The Bengal Tenancy Act of 1885 enacted by the British government was largely a response to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Pabna Revolt (1873–1876)** was an important peasant movement that took place in the Pabna district of Bengal (now in Bangladesh) against the oppressive practices of zamindars under British rule.
● **The revolt was mainly caused by the illegal increase of rents, forced collection of extra** **cesses (abwabs), and frequent evictions carried out by zamindars after the introduction of** **the Permanent Settlement**, which had made them the owners of land.
● The peasants, known as ryots, organized themselves into Ryot Leagues and launched a peaceful and legal struggle by refusing to pay illegal rents and by filing cases in courts against unjust landlords. Unlike many violent uprisings, the Pabna Revolt **remained largely non-violent and constitutional**, which gained it sympathy even from some British officials.
● The movement was led by local educated peasants like Ishwar Chandra Roy, Shambhu Pal, and Keshab Chandra Roy, and it spread over a wide area of eastern Bengal.
● As a result of the pressure created by this movement, the British government later passed the **Bengal** **Tenancy Act of 1885,** which provided greater security of tenure and rent protection to peasants.
● The **Bengal Tenancy Act of 1885** was enacted by the British government to provide legal protection to peasants in Bengal against the exploitation of zamindars, and it was largely a response to peasant movements like the **Pabna Revolt**.
● The Act aimed to give greater security to tenants by recognizing that any ryot who had occupied land continuously for **twelve years** would enjoy **permanent tenancy** and could not be evicted without legal reason. It also restricted the power of zamindars to raise rents arbitrarily, allowing rent increases only under clearly defined conditions.
● The Act abolished the collection of **abwabs**, or illegal extra charges, which zamindars had been forcing peasants to pay. In addition, tenants were given the right to **inherit, transfer, sell or mortgage** their land, which strengthened their economic position.
● The **Bengal Tenancy Act of 1885 did not abolish the Permanent Settlement**. The **Permanent** **Settlement of 1793** continued to remain in force in Bengal. It was gradually abolished by various laws in post-Independence India. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Commission Headed by Recommendation
I.
First Law
Commission of
India (1834)
Thomas
Babington
Macaulay
Indian Penal
Code (IPC)
II.
Army
Reorganisation
Commission
(1859)
Richard
Strachey
Increasing the
number of Indian
soldiers
III.
Famine
Commission
(1880)
Jonathan
Peel
Formulation of
Famine Codes
IV.
Education
Commission
(1882)
William
Hunter
Primary
education should
be placed under
the control of
local bodies In which of the above rows is the given information **not** correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Macaulay Commission** refers to the **First Law Commission of India** (1834), which was headed by Lord Thomas Babington Macaulay during the Governor-Generalship of Lord William Bentinck. The main task of the Commission was to codify Indian laws. Its most important achievement was **drafting the Indian Penal Code (IPC)** in 1837. Although the IPC was finally enforced only in 1860, it became the foundation of India’s criminal law system. The Commission also laid the groundwork for other major legal codes like the Criminal Procedure Code and Civil Procedure Code. **So, row I is** **correctly matched.**
● **Army Reorganisation Commission (1859):** After the **Revolt of 1857**, this commission restructured the Indian army by increasing the number of **European soldiers** and reducing Indian troops to prevent future rebellions. The **Army Reorganisation Commission (1859)** is also known as the **Peel Commission.** It was named after **Sir Jonathan Peel**, who was the **British Secretary of** **State for War** and chaired the commission. It ensured stronger British control and prevented future rebellions.  **So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Famine Commission of 1880** is also known as the **Strachey Commission.** It was named after **Sir Richard Strachey**, who was its chairman. The **Strachey Commission** examined the causes of the **Great Famine of 1876–78** and recommended the formulation of **Famine Codes**, improved relief works, and better food distribution to prevent mass starvation in the future.  **So, row III is not** **correctly matched.**
● The **Education Commission of 1882**, popularly known as the **Hunter Commission**, was appointed by **Lord Ripon** to review the working of **Wood’s Despatch (1854)**, which had laid the foundation of modern education in India. The Commission headed by **Sir William Hunter,** focused mainly on **primary and secondary education**. It recommended that **primary education** should be expanded and placed under the control of **local bodies** like municipalities and district boards. It also suggested that **secondary education** should be increasingly handed over to **private Indian management**, while the government should concentrate more on **higher education**. **So, row IV is correctly** **matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Mughal paintings are known for their realistic portrayal and courtly themes.
II. 	 Rajput paintings emphasise bold colours, local legends, and devotional themes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Miniature painting in India flourished between the 16th and 19th centuries under the patronage of various regional kingdoms and the Mughal Empire. These small-scale, highly detailed paintings were used to illustrate manuscripts, albums, and literary works.
● **Mughal miniature paintings combined Persian artistic techniques** with Indian themes. They are **characterised by fine brushwork, attention to detail, realistic depictions of human figures** **and nature**, and themes revolving around the court, battles, hunting, and royal life. This synthesis created a distinctive style that documented the historical and cultural life of the Mughal period. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Rajput paintings**, including styles like Mewar, Bundi, and Jaipur, are marked by **vibrant colours**, flat perspectives, and expressive figures. They depict scenes from epics such as the **Ramayana and** **Mahabharata, local folklore, and devotional themes** associated with Krishna and other deities, reflecting the religious and cultural identity of Rajput courts. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Miniature paintings were predominantly created on paper, often in albums** (muraqqa), manuscripts, or palm leaves, using opaque watercolours. Fresco painting on walls was a separate tradition seen in temples and forts, but it does not define Mughal or Rajput miniature painting. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Land revenue policy
Implemented in
Associated Governor General
I. - Permanent Settlement Eastern India Lord Cornwallis
II. - Ryotwari system Southern India Thomas Munro
III. Mahalwari system
NorthWestern India
Lord Hastings
In how many of the rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
 The land revenue policies of the British in India were designed mainly to secure a **regular and high**
**income** for the colonial government rather than to improve agriculture or the condition of the peasants. To achieve this, the British introduced three major systems—the **Permanent Settlement, Ryotwari** **System, and Mahalwari System**.  Under the **Permanent Settlement** introduced by **Governor General Lord Cornwallis** in 1793 in
**Bengal, Bihar and Orissa,** the zamindars were made the owners of land and the revenue was fixed permanently. While this created a class of loyal landlords, it led to severe exploitation of peasants, who were reduced to tenants and had no security.  **So, row I is correctly matched.**  The **Ryotwari system**, introduced by **Thomas Munro (Governor of Madras)** in **Madras and**
**Bombay,** recognized the peasants (ryots) as landowners and made them pay revenue directly to the government, but the revenue rates were extremely high, often up to half of the produce, which pushed farmers into debt and dependence on moneylenders. **Governor-General at that time was** **Lord** **Hastings (1813–1823). So, row II is not correctly matched.**  Under the **Mahalwari system**, followed in North India and Punjab, the village as a whole was made
responsible for paying land revenue, but frequent revisions and heavy demands placed great pressure on rural communities. Overall, these land revenue systems led to the **commercialization of agriculture**, widespread **indebtedness**, **loss of land by peasants**, and recurring **famines**, and they became a major cause of rural unrest and peasant revolts against British rule. **It was introduced by** **Holt Mackenzie** (1822) and the **Governor-General was** **Lord Hastings. So, row III is correctly matched.** **So, in only two of the rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following features:
I. - The walls were richly decorated with carvings and geometrical patterns
II. 	 Gopurams were constructed on all sides
III. 	 Rock pillars were monolithic
IV. 	 Temple pillars often feature the mythical creature Yali engraved on them
Which of the above are found in the Vijayanagara School of temple architecture?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Vijayanagara Empire (c. 1336–1646 CE)** in South India is renowned for its distinctive style of temple architecture. Temples built under this school **combined the earlier Chalukya, Hoysala, and Pandya** **traditions** with innovations that reflected the empire’s grandeur and devotion to Hinduism.
● **Vijayanagara temples feature elaborate wall carvings depicting scenes from Hindu epics,** **deities, and ornamental geometric designs**. These carvings were not only decorative but also served to narrate mythological stories and highlight the temple’s religious significance. Temples like the **Virupaksha and Vittala** in Hampi exemplify this elaborate wall ornamentation. **So, point I is correct.**
● **In the Vijayanagara school of temple architecture, gopurams, towered gateways, were** **typically built on all cardinal directions of the temple complex**, providing multiple entrances and emphasising the grandeur of the structure. The main gopuram, usually on the eastern side, was the tallest and most elaborately decorated. Secondary gopurams on the other sides maintained symmetry, facilitated access for different purposes. Temples like Virupaksha and Vittala in Hampi clearly demonstrate this feature, with multiple gopurams strategically placed around the temple precinct. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● **A hallmark of Vijayanagara architecture is the use of monolithic pillars**—single pieces of stone carved to form massive, stable columns. Many of these pillars feature elaborate designs, and some, such as the **musical pillars in the Vittala Temple**, produce musical notes when struck. **So, point III is** **correct.**
● **Yali, a mythical lion-like creature with elements of an elephant or horse, is commonly** **carved on pillars of Vijayanagara temples.** It symbolises strength and protection and is a recurring motif in the empire’s architectural style, reflecting both artistic excellence and symbolic significance. **So,** **point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
