questions.questionText: Which one of the following Five Year Plan, for the first time, explicitly affirmed 'self-reliance' (Atmanirbharta) as a primary objective of development?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● The concept of **self-reliance** was first chalked out in the **Fourth Five Year Plan (1969 – 1974)** as a primary objective under the **theme of "Growth with Stability and Progressive Achievement of** **Self-Reliance**. Its primary goal was to reduce dependence on foreign aid (specifically food aid under PL480) and promote indigenous technology.
● **The Sixth five-year plan (**1980–1985) was launched under the theme of “Direct Attack on Poverty.” It focused on poverty eradication through focus on infrastructure, and the "Integrated Rural Development Programme" (IRDP). It marked the beginning of economic liberalization.
● The **Eighth Five-Year Plan (1992–1997)**, launched post-1991 reforms under Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao, marked a major shift towards a market-driven economy, emphasizing liberalization, privatization, and globalization (LPG). It focused on human development, employment generation, and infrastructure development. It has a primary **theme of Human Resource Development.**
● The **Twelfth five-year plan** was launched for 2012–2017 and had theme of Faster, More Inclusive, and Sustainable Growth. This was the last Five-Year Plan before the Planning Commission was replaced by NITI Aayog. **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Reserve Bank of India’s (RBI) Liquidity Management Framework, consider the following statements:
I. - It is used to manage short-term capital of banks by limiting the volatility in inter-bank call money markets.
II. 	 Marginal Standing Facility provides a safety valve for banks to manage unanticipated liquidity shocks after exhausting their regular Repo limits.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● A **liquidity management framework (LMF)** was provisioned by the RBI in 2014 to check volatility in the inter-bank call money market (CMM) and allow banks to manage their needs of short-term capital. At LMF core, it relies on the Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) that is the repo and reverse repo mechanism through which RBI injects or absorbs liquidity as needed. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Marginal Standing Facility (MSF)** was specifically designed to act as a safety valve for banks to manage unanticipated liquidity shocks. If a bank has exhausted its borrowing limit under the regular Repo window, it can borrow overnight from the RBI under MSF.
o After exhausting the option of the various repos, banks can borrow upto 1 per cent of their
NDTL directly from the RBI for one day (called overnight) under the marginal standing facility (MSF). **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Reserve Bank had constituted an Internal Working Group (IWG) to review the extant Liquidity Management Framework.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Services
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
High Wholesale Price Index always leads to high
Consumer Price Index.
**Statement II:**
The Consumer Price Index measures prices paid
by consumers.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● The **Wholesale Price Index** measures price changes at the wholesale or producer level, mainly for goods, while the **Consumer Price Index** measures prices paid by consumers and includes services like housing, education, and healthcare. Because their baskets and weights differ, a rise in WPI may or may not pass through fully to CPI. **So, statement II is correct.**
● For example, if global crude oil or metal prices rise, WPI may increase sharply because it includes fuel and industrial inputs. But CPI may rise only slightly if government taxes are reduced, subsidies are provided, or retailers absorb part of the cost. Similarly, food prices may rise at the wholesale level due to supply gluts or export bans, but retail prices may remain stable due to buffer stocks or price controls.
● In general, WPI affects CPI through **cost-push transmission**—higher input costs eventually raise retail prices—but this depends on factors like demand conditions, government policy, market competition, and supply chains. So, high WPI can lead to high CPI, but **not always and not immediately**. **So,** **statement I is correct.** **So, Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India’s statistical system is undergoing a comprehensive modernization to better capture the realities of a fast-changing economy.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Finance Commission
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian economy, consider
the following statements:
I. - Fiscal Deficit represents the total borrowing
requirements of the Government of India.
II. 	 The Ways and Means Advances from the
Reserve Bank of India are long term loans to
finance the deficit.
III. 	 The fiscal deficit as a percentage of Gross
Domestic Product has shown a consistent
declining trend over the last five years.
IV. 	 Deficit financing is economically sustainable
when it is utilized to fund higher revenue
gaps.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● When the balance of the government’s total receipts (revenue + capital receipts) and total expenditures (revenue + capital expenditures) turns out to be negative, it shows the situation of fiscal deficit. The situation of fiscal deficit indicates that the government is spending beyond its means. Thus, the **fiscal** **deficit represents the total borrowing requirements** of the Government of India. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The act/process of financing/supporting a deficit budget by a government is deficit financing. **Ways and** **Means Advances (WMA) are temporary, short-term loan facilities provided by the Reserve** **Bank of India (RBI)** to the Central and State governments to bridge temporary mismatches between their receipts and payments. The size and cost of WMA would be determined on the basis of mutual agreement. Amounts drawn beyond the WMA limit would be treated as overdraft. **So, statement II** **is not correct.**
● **After the fiscal deficit** spiked to 9.2% of GDP in FY 2020-21 due to the pandemic, it has shown a consistent declining trend. The fiscal deficit in Budget Estimate 2026-27 is estimated to be 4.3 percent of GDP.
o **Financial Year (FY)** 2021-22: 6.7% o FY 2022-23: 6.4% o FY 2023-24: 5.6% o FY 2024-25 (RE): 4.8% o FY 2025-26 (BE): Targeted below 4.5% (approx. 4.4%). **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Deficit financing** is considered economically sustainable when **utilized for Capital Expenditure** **(building assets like roads, ports, and railways)** because these assets generate future revenue and boost GDP growth. Utilizing deficit financing to fund Revenue Expenditure (salaries, pensions, and interest payments) is unsustainable, as it consumes borrowed money for day-to-day operations without creating productive capacity for repayment. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India's fiscal deficit for the first nine months of this fiscal year through December stood at Rs 8.55 lakh crore rupees, or 54.5% of annual estimates.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), consider the following statements:
I. - It helps banks absorb a reasonable amount of loss before they become insolvent.
II. 	 In India, CAR requirements for both public and private sector banks are the same.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● The **Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR),** also known as the **Capital-to-Risk Weighted Assets Ratio** **(CRAR)**, is the **ratio of a bank's capital to its Risk-Weighted Assets (RWA)**. CAR is critical to ensure that banks have a large enough financial cushion to absorb a reasonable amount of losses before they become insolvent. It was adopted at the Basel (Switzerland) accord in 1988, at a meeting of the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Reserve Bank of India mandates that commercial banks maintain a minimum Capital to RiskWeighted Assets Ratio of 9%, which is stricter than the global Basel III norm of 8%. When including the required 2.5% Capital Conservation Buffer (CCB), the effective minimum CAR for Indian banks rises to
11.5%.
● In India, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) mandates a minimum Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), or Capital to Risk-weighted Assets Ratio (CRAR), of 9% for all scheduled commercial banks (including private sector banks) to ensure financial stability. **Public sector banks are required to maintain a stricter,** **higher CAR of 12%** to provide a greater cushion against stressed assets. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Calculating CAR**
● The capital adequacy ratio is calculated by dividing a bank's capital by its riskweighted assets. Under the current Basel rules, banks must meet the following requirements at all times:
● Common Equity Tier 1 must be at least 4.5% of risk-weighted assets (RWA).
● Tier 1 capital must be at least 6% of RWA.
● Total capital must be at least 8.0% of RWA.
**Relevance:** RBI has observed in its latest financial stability report that none of the banks would fall short of the minimum CRAR requirement even under the adverse scenarios.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Services
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - National security
II. 	 Energy security
III. 	 Provision of financial services
IV. 	 Availability of important minerals
V. 	 Critical infrastructure
How many of the above criteria are used to delineate Strategic Sectors in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● **Disinvestment** means the sale or liquidation of assets by the government, usually Central and state public sector enterprises, projects, or other fixed assets. The Department of Disinvestment was set up as a separate Department on 10 December 1999 under the Ministry of Finance.
o The Department of Disinvestment was renamed as the Department of Investment and Public
Asset Management (DIPAM) from 14 April 2016. It deals with managing the central government’s investments, and stake of the Central govt equity.
● The **New Public Sector Enterprise (PSE) Policy for Atma Nirbhar Bharat**, which also provides overall guidance on strategic disinvestment, was notified on 4th February, 2021.
● **The New PSE policy delineates Strategic sectors based on the criteria** of: o **National security, energy security, critical infrastructure, provision of financial**
**services and availability of important minerals**. So, point I, II, III, IV and are correct
● As per the **New PSE Policy, the government will retain only a bare minimum presence at** **the holding company level** in the strategic sectors**. All others will be privatized, merged, or** **closed.** All PSEs in non-strategic sectors shall be considered for privatization, where feasible, such enterprises shall be considered for closure. **However, the policy does not apply to** certain classes of public sector entities such as Not-for-profit companies, or CPSEs providing support to vulnerable groups, or having developmental/promotional roles etc. **So, all the above criteria are used to delineate Strategic Sectors.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Modes of Disinvestment**
● **Initial/ Further Public Offer (IPO/ FPO)**: When an unlisted company makes a fresh issue of shares for sale for the first time to the public, it is called an IPO.
o When an already listed company makes either a fresh issue of shares to the
public or an offer for sale to the public, it is called an FPO.
● **Exchange Traded Fund (ETF):** An ETF is a basket of stocks that reflects the composition of an Index.
● **Offer for Sale (OFS):** It is a simpler method of share sale through the exchange platform for listed companies. The mechanism was first introduced by SEBI in 2012.
● **Buyback** **of shares**: Buyback is the repurchase by a company of its shares from the existing shareholders that reduces the number of its shares in the open market.
● **Strategic disinvestment:** It implies the sale of a substantial portion of the Government shareholding of a CPSE of up to 50 per cent or such a higher percentage, as the competent authority may determine, along with transfer of management control.
**Relevance:** Confederation of Indian Industry (CII) has suggested an accelerated four-pronged strategy to unlock value from the disinvestment of public sector enterprises.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Natural Farming:
I. - It is a chemical-free farming that integrates local livestock in farming methods.
II. 	 It employs microbial coatings for seeds to protect crops against soil-borne diseases.
III. 	 The National Mission on Natural Farming was launched to support farmers to selfcertify their products as naturally grown.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Agriculture Ministry defines natural farming as a “**chemical-free” farming system that only** **uses inputs produced using livestock and plant resources.** NF follows local agro-ecological principles rooted in local knowledge, location-specific technologies and is evolved as per the local agroecology. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Bijamrita (Beejamrit)** is a microbial coating method. The coating is made from cow dung, urine, and lime, used for treating seeds and seedlings. This provides a protective layer against fungi and soil-borne or seed-borne diseases during the early stages of growth. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **National Mission on Natural Farming** (NMNF) is a standalone Centrally Sponsored Scheme under the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers’ Welfare. It is an improvement of the Bhartiya Prakritik Krishi Paddhti (BPKP). The Mission has a target of initiating natural farming in 7.5 Lakh ha area across 15,000 clusters, setting up 10,000 need-based Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRC) for easy availability of NF bioinputs and generating awareness among 1 crore farmers on Natural Farming. Further, farmers are supported by a simple certification system and common national brand for naturally grown chemical free produce. **But it does not have any component to promote self certification by farmers.**
○ **Self-certification is** promoted under the Participatory Guarantee System (PGS-India), which is a peer-based, community-focused certification system. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Andhra Pradesh cuts chemical fertiliser use by 2.28%, seeks more Centre support for natural farming.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - During recession, economic downturn is more severe and prolonged than during depression.
II. 	 During depression, disinflation while during recession the economy faces deflation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● As per the the **World Bank and IMF** when any economy observe a fall in its GDP for two consecutive quarters it is said to be hit with **technical** **recession**. A **Depression** is not just a long recession; it is a more catastrophic downturn characterized by its depth (often a **decline in GDP of 10% or more**) and its duration (lasting several years).
o A depression is a dramatic and sustained downturn in economic activity, with symptoms including
a sharp fall in economic growth, employment, and production. It's a recession that lasts longer than three years or that results in a decline of at least 10% in annual GDP. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
o In case of **recession,** inflation remains lower or may show further signs of falling down. **During a**
**depression**, the economy typically faces **deflation (falling prices)** or very low inflation. This is because demand collapses so severely that businesses must lower prices to attract buyers. **So, statement II is** **not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** US hiring rate falls to 3.3%, matching COVID-era lows and signaling Recession fears.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Land reforms in India were aimed to improve efficiency and productivity in agriculture.
**Statement II:** The traditional agrarian system extracted rent without investing in agricultural productivity. **Statement III:** Surplus land above ceiling limit was acquired by the State for redistribution.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India the objectives of land reforms were officially formulated for the first time in the **First Five Year** **Plan.** The main objectives were removal of impediments to agricultural production and creating conditions for evolving an agrarian economy with high levels of efficiency and productivity. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The high inequality in land ownership not only had a negative economic impact on the economy; but it was badly intertwined with the caste system in India and the allocation of social prestige and status by the society at large.
o Legal reforms like abolition of the zamindari system were taken, which removed the layer of
intermediaries who stood between the cultivators and the state. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The ceilings on landholdings referred to legally stipulating the maximum size beyond which no individual farmer or farm household could hold any land. In 1942 the **Kumarappan Committee** recommended the maximum size of lands a landlord can retain. It was three times the economic holding i.e. sufficient livelihood for a family. By 1961-62, all the state governments had passed the land ceiling acts. But the ceiling limits varied from state to state.
o As a result of the land reform policy of the first phase (during 1950-65), out of a total cultivated
area of about 400 million acres a mere 2.3 million acres was declared surplus. o In 1972, national guidelines were issued with ceiling limits varying from region to region,
depending on the kind of land, its productivity, and other such factors. o It was 10-18 acres for best land, 18-27 acres for second class land and for the rest with 27-54
acres of land with a slightly higher limit in the hill and desert areas. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Financial Markets
questions.questionText: In the context of banking and finance, which one of the following best describes the term "Haircut"?

options.a: The penalty imposed on banks for failing to meet Priority Sector Lending targets
options.b: The reduction in the interest rate offered by commercial banks to their most valued customers during a recession
options.c: It describes the amount of money a lender forgoes during the loan resolution process.
options.d: The tax deduction applied to the dividend income of retail investors in the stock market

questions.explanationMarkdown:
 In banking, this statement describes a “haircut.” **A haircut is the portion of the loan amount that** **a lender agrees to waive during the resolution** of a stressed or non-performing loan. When a borrower cannot repay fully, banks often accept a reduced payment under restructuring, settlement, or insolvency proceedings to recover at least part of the money instead of losing everything. The amount they give up is called the haircut.
 For example, if a bank lent ₹100 crore and accepts ₹70 crore in settlement, the remaining ₹30 crore is the haircut. This represents the financial sacrifice or loss borne by the lender to close the loan account. Haircuts are common in insolvency cases handled under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, where creditors approve a resolution plan that may involv**e partial repayment depending on the** **borrower’s asset value and recovery prospects.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Banks take 67% haircut in 1,194 insolvency cases have recovered Rs 3.89 lakh crore against admitted claims of Rs 12 lakh crore through resolution plans under the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Cryptocurrency, which of the following statements are **not** correct?
I.  	 The Reserve Bank of India regulates cryptocurrencies as currency.
II. 	 They can be converted into fiat money through crypto exchanges or peer-to-peer platforms.
III. 	 The value of cryptocurrencies does not fluctuate due to volatility in the international currency market.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● A **cryptocurrency** is a medium of exchange, such as the rupee or the US dollar, but is digital in format and uses encryption techniques to both control the creation of monetary units and to verify the exchange of money. It works on blockchain technology.
● India does not have a single consolidated cryptocurrency law. Instead, crypto regulation is shaped through existing financial, tax and anti-money laundering laws. **The Reserve Bank of India does not** **regulate cryptocurrencies as currency.** It regulates banks and financial institutions that interact with crypto businesses. After the Supreme Court struck down the earlier banking restriction, regulated entities are permitted to provide services to crypto platforms subject to due diligence. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **Cryptocurrencies can be converted into fiat money (like INR, USD, etc.) through crypto** **exchanges or peer-to-peer platforms.** For example, users can sell Bitcoin on an exchange and receive money in their bank account. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Cryptocurrency prices are highly volatile.** Their value fluctuates due to demand–supply dynamics, regulatory news, speculation, liquidity conditions, and global financial sentiment. While they are not directly tied to international currency markets, they still show large price swings. **So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Donald Trump recently vowed to make the United States the world’s crypto capital.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Non-performing assets are those which if it has not been serviced for a period of less than or equal to 12 months.
II. 	 The Agriculture sector has the highest share in the Gross Non-Performing Assets ratio of Public Sector Banks.
III. 	 The National Asset Reconstruction Company Limited is established by a consortium of banks to aggregate and resolve stressed assets of banks.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● According to the RBI, an asset becomes a **Non-Performing Asset (NPA)** when it ceases to generate income for the bank, specifically when interest or installments of principal remain overdue for a period of more than 90 days. In the case of agriculture, loans for short-duration crops become NPAs after two, and long-duration crops after one, crop season. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **NPAs were classified into three types**: o Sub-standard: remaining NPAs for less than or equal to 12 months; o Doubtful: remaining NPAs for more than 12 months;
o Loss assets: where the loss has been identified by the bank or internal/external auditors or the
RBI inspection, but the amount has not been written off.
● **Gross NPA (GNPA)** is the total amount of loans a bank has issued that have become non-performing, meaning borrowers haven't paid principal or interest for over 90 days, before any deductions for provisions (money set aside for potential losses) are made.
o As per the **Economic Survey 2025-26** the **GNPA** ratio for the industry decreased from 2.3%
in March 2025 to 1.9% in September 2025. For services, the ratio decreased from 2.0% to 1.8%, and for personal loans, it decreased from 1.2% to 1.1%. In contrast, the GNPA ratio for the agriculture sector remains relatively higher at 6.0% in September 2025 and has improved marginally from 6.1% in March 2025. **So, statement II is correct.**
● National Asset Reconstruction Company Limited (**NARCL) is a government entity that** was incorporated on 7th July 2021 with 51% **stake held by Public Sector Banks** and balance by private banks with Canara Bank being the sponsor bank. It is registered with the Reserve Bank of India as an Asset Reconstruction Company under the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002.
o The mandate of NARCL is to acquire stressed assets (non-performing assets) of ₹500 crore and
above from banks from various commercial banks and manage their resolution through the IDRCL (India Debt Resolution Company Limited). **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Gross NPAs of Scheduled Commercial Banks (SCBs) for domestic operations reach a historic low of 2.15% as of September, 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Dumping in international trade:
I. - Dumping is the export of a product at a lower price in foreign markets than in domestic markets.
II. 	 It is completely prohibited under the rules of the World Trade Organisation (WTO).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
 **Dumping occurs when a producer sells a product in a foreign market at a price lower than**
**its normal value, often below the domestic price or cost of production. The difference in** **pricing is not due to legitimate cost factors** such as transportation or tariffs but is aimed at gaining an unfair competitive advantage in the importing country. **So, statement I is correct.**  The objective of dumping is typically to capture foreign markets, eliminate competition, or dispose of
surplus production.  **Dumping is not outright prohibited under WTO rules**. The WTO allows dumping to occur as
long as it does not cause injury to the domestic industry of the importing country. However, when dumping causes “material injury” to a domestic industry, member countries are permitted to take antidumping measures such as imposing anti-dumping duties to offset the margin of dumping. These measures must follow due procedure, including investigation and notification as per the WTO Anti-Dumping Agreement. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Stablecoins seek to maintain a stable value by pegging their market value to an external reference asset.
**Statement II:** Stablecoins are legally guaranteed to maintain a fixed value, ensuring they are immune to market volatility and reserve shortages.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Stablecoins are a type of cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to another asset, such as a fiat currency or gold, to maintain a stable price.
● **Stablecoins represent a specific category of digital currency designed to mitigate price** **volatility by anchoring their market value to an external reference asset**. This pegging mechanism typically utilizes fiat currencies, such as the U.S. Dollar, or commodities like gold to ensure price stability. By providing a steady valuation, stablecoins aim to overcome the inherent fluctuations associated with conventional cryptocurrencies, thereby facilitating their use as a reliable medium of exchange for everyday economic transactions. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Despite their name, stablecoins offer no absolute legal or technical certainty of value** **maintenance**, as the "de-pegging" phenomenon remains a documented systemic risk. While designed for stability, these assets are subject to secondary market forces where supply-and-demand fluctuations can drive prices away from the intended peg. Furthermore, the stability of these coins is entirely dependent on the adequacy and liquidity of their underlying reserves; if these assets are insufficient or cannot be quickly converted to cash during a surge in withdrawals, the issuer will fail to satisfy redemption requests, potentially leading to a total loss of value for the holder. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In 2025, stablecoins transitioned from being "experimental" to a mature pillar of global finance. Partly due to the passage of the GENIUS Act in the United States.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Agriculture (Schemes, APMC, MSP, Exports, etc.)
questions.questionText: In the context of economic reforms, liberalization refers to:

options.a: Complete withdrawal of the State from productive economic activities
options.b: Gradual reduction of government controls to allow greater role of market forces
options.c: Replacement of public sector enterprises with private monopolies
options.d: Elimination of all forms of regulation in domestic and external trade

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Liberalization means **easing excessive state controls** such as: industrial licensing, price controls, quantitative restrictions, entry and exit barriers. That is, **gradual reduction of government controls** **to allow greater role of market forces.** The objective is **not to remove the State**, but to **shift** **its role** from direct controller/producer to **facilitator and regulator**. Markets are allowed greater freedom in: investment decisions, production, pricing, trade. This is why liberalization is often described as **“reform of controls, not removal of the State.” So, option (b) is correct.**
● Complete withdrawal of the State from productive economic activities Liberalization does **not** require the State to exit all productive sectors. Even after liberalization the government continues in **infrastructure, defence, social sectors**, and strategic industries. This option describes an **extreme** **laissez-faire system**, not liberalization. **So, option (a) is not correct.**
● Replacement of public sector enterprises with private monopolies Liberalization encourages **competition**, not monopolies. While privatization may occur, liberalization aims to: reduce inefficiency, increase competition, and improve consumer choice. Monopoly (public or private) contradicts the **core** **logic of liberalization**. **So, option (c) is not correct.**
● Elimination of all forms of regulation in domestic and external trade. Liberalization involves **deregulation**, not **de-regulation to zero**. Regulations continue for: consumer protection, environmental safeguards, financial stability, fair competition. Completely eliminating regulation would lead to **market failures**, which liberalization explicitly seeks to avoid. **So, option (d) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
